title: What is JVM, JRE, and JDK?
answer: JVM (Java Virtual Machine) executes Java bytecode and provides platform independence. JRE (Java Runtime Environment) includes the JVM plus libraries needed to run Java applications. JDK (Java Development Kit) includes the JRE plus development tools like the compiler (javac), used to build Java applications.

title: Why is Java called platform-independent?
answer: Java source code compiles into bytecode (.class files) rather than machine code. This bytecode runs on any device with a compatible JVM, so the same compiled file runs unchanged on Windows, Linux, or macOS — following the 'write once, run anywhere' principle.

title: What are the primitive data types in Java?
answer: Java has eight primitive types: byte, short, int, long (integers), float, double (decimals), char (single character), and boolean (true/false). Unlike objects, primitives store actual values directly in memory.

title: What is the difference between '==' and '.equals()' for objects?
answer: '==' compares references (whether two variables point to the same memory location), while '.equals()' compares logical content and can be overridden by classes to define custom equality, as String and most wrapper classes do.

title: What is a constructor in Java?
answer: A constructor is a special method with the same name as the class, used to initialize objects when they're created. It has no return type, and Java provides a default no-argument constructor if none is defined explicitly.

title: What is the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
answer: String is immutable — every modification creates a new object. StringBuilder is mutable and faster but not thread-safe, ideal for single-threaded string manipulation. StringBuffer is mutable and thread-safe (synchronized methods), used when thread safety is required, at a small performance cost.

title: Explain the difference between method overloading and overriding with an example.
answer: Overloading means multiple methods share a name but differ in parameters within the same class (resolved at compile time). Overriding means a subclass redefines a method inherited from its parent with the exact same signature (resolved at runtime via dynamic dispatch).

title: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface in modern Java?
answer: An abstract class can hold state (instance variables) and a mix of abstract and concrete methods, and a class can extend only one. An interface (since Java 8) can have default and static methods but no instance state, and a class can implement multiple interfaces — useful for defining contracts across unrelated classes.

title: What is the purpose of the 'final' keyword?
answer: 'final' applied to a variable makes it a constant (cannot be reassigned), to a method prevents it from being overridden by subclasses, and to a class prevents it from being subclassed at all, as with Java's String class.

title: What is garbage collection in Java?
answer: Garbage collection is the JVM's automatic process of reclaiming memory occupied by objects that are no longer reachable from any active reference, freeing developers from manual memory management and preventing most memory leaks common in languages like C++.

title: What is exception handling and what's the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?
answer: Exception handling manages runtime errors using try, catch, finally, and throw/throws. Checked exceptions (like IOException) must be declared or caught at compile time, representing recoverable conditions. Unchecked exceptions (like NullPointerException) extend RuntimeException and aren't compiler-enforced, usually indicating programming bugs.

title: What are the differences between ArrayList and LinkedList?
answer: ArrayList uses a dynamic array internally, giving fast O(1) random access but slower O(n) insertions/deletions in the middle. LinkedList uses doubly-linked nodes, giving fast O(1) insertions/deletions at known positions but slower O(n) random access.

title: What is the difference between HashMap, LinkedHashMap, and TreeMap?
answer: HashMap offers O(1) average access with no guaranteed order. LinkedHashMap maintains insertion order while keeping HashMap's performance. TreeMap keeps keys sorted (natural or custom order) using a Red-Black tree, giving O(log n) operations.

title: What is multithreading, and how do you create a thread in Java?
answer: Multithreading allows concurrent execution of two or more parts of a program to maximize CPU utilization. You can create a thread by extending the Thread class or implementing the Runnable interface and passing it to a Thread object.

title: What is the Java Memory Model and how does it relate to the Heap and Stack?
answer: The Stack stores method call frames and local primitive variables, with memory automatically freed when a method returns. The Heap stores all objects and is managed by the garbage collector. The Java Memory Model further defines rules around visibility and ordering of variable reads/writes across threads, which is why keywords like 'volatile' and 'synchronized' matter in concurrent code.

title: What is the difference between synchronized methods and synchronized blocks?
answer: A synchronized method locks the entire method using the object's (or class's, if static) intrinsic lock for its full duration. A synchronized block locks only a specific critical section using an explicit object as the lock, giving finer-grained control and better performance when only part of a method needs protection.

title: Explain how HashMap works internally.
answer: HashMap stores key-value pairs in an array of buckets. A key's hashCode() determines its bucket index. Collisions (multiple keys in the same bucket) are handled via a linked list, which converts to a balanced tree (Red-Black tree) once a bucket exceeds a threshold (8 entries in modern Java) for better worst-case lookup performance.

title: What are Java Streams and how do they differ from Collections?
answer: A Stream (java.util.stream) represents a pipeline of operations (map, filter, reduce) applied to a data source, evaluated lazily and often in a functional style, without storing elements itself. Collections are in-memory data structures that store elements; Streams are consumed once and are typically built from a Collection.

title: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?
answer: Fail-fast iterators (like those from ArrayList, HashMap) throw a ConcurrentModificationException if the underlying collection is structurally modified during iteration. Fail-safe iterators (like those from ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList) operate on a cloned or snapshot copy, allowing safe modification during iteration but without reflecting the change immediately.

title: What are functional interfaces and lambda expressions in Java 8+?
answer: A functional interface has exactly one abstract method (e.g., Runnable, Comparator) and can be implemented concisely using a lambda expression instead of an anonymous class, enabling a more functional programming style.
example:
Comparator<String> byLength = (a, b) -> a.length() - b.length();
list.sort(byLength);
